one_line_takeaway: An attacker-centric survey that organizes poisoning attacks on recommender systems into a four-stage pipeline (goal / capability / victim architecture / strategy) and defenses into pre-training filtering vs. robust training, cataloguing ~48 attack and ~46 defense papers and the metrics used to evaluate them.
key_finding: The central takeaway is the attacker-perspective pipeline as an organizing lens: framing poisoning attacks as a sequence of goal -> capability -> victim-architecture -> strategy decisions both aligns with how real attacks are constructed and doubles as a taxonomy that pinpoints where each existing attack focuses. On the defense side, the paper argues for a 'defense-in-depth' split between pre-training data filtering and in-training robustness, and highlights three structural gaps in the field: (i) security research lags the fast evolution of RS (e.g., self-supervised and LLM-based recommenders); (ii) attack and defense research are 'out of sync,' often assuming weak adversaries and thus underestimating real threats; and (iii) a benchmarking shortfall, since existing secure-RS platforms (SDLib, RecAD, ARLib) lack comprehensive coverage. Emerging future directions it flags include cross-platform manipulation, 'cultural' attacks on multilingual RS, stronger theoretical foundations for the cost/impact economics of poisoning, long-term (diluted-over-time) effects, and data-neutralization defenses.
core_contribution: This is a survey (IJCAI-style, arXiv 2406.01022v2, cs.CR) that organizes the literature on poisoning attacks and defenses in recommender systems (RS) from an attacker-centric perspective. Its own contribution is not any new attack or defense method, but a unifying taxonomy in the form of a four-stage 'poisoning attack pipeline': (1) setting attack goals, (2) assessing attacker capability, (3) analyzing victim architecture, and (4) implementing poisoning strategies. Each stage is decomposed: goals into system degradation (untargeted) vs. targeted manipulation (plus hybrid-goal attacks); capability into knowledge, cost, and invisibility constraints; victim architecture into centralized vs. decentralized (e.g., federated) RS; and strategy into data poisoning vs. model (gradient) poisoning. Correspondingly, the authors propose a two-branch defense taxonomy: (a) poisoning data filtering before training (subdivided into supervised anomaly filtering, unsupervised clustering filtering, and confidence-based filtering) and (b) robust training during/after training (subdivided into collaborative enhancement training, confidence-aware training, and contextual enrichment training). The survey also catalogs standard evaluation metrics and lays out limitations and future directions. Note: named methods such as Infmix, FedAttack, ClusterAttack, UA-FedRec, PoisonRec, A-hum, RAPU, GTA, AutoAttack, and GSPAttack are prior work catalogued and slotted into the taxonomy (summarized in Table 1), not contributions of this paper; likewise prior surveys (Fan et al. 2022 on trustworthy RS, Anelli et al. 2021 on robust RS, Si and Li 2020 on heuristic attacks, Deldjoo et al. 2021 on adversarial-learning attacks, Rezaimehr and Dadkhah 2021 on detection) are the related work the authors position against.
threat_model: The paper's subject IS a threat model, which it formalizes: an attacker controls a subset of malicious/fake users U_M who perform strategic interactions (and may alter item features), injecting a malicious dataset D_M into the RS training data, or in decentralized/federated settings upload poisoned gradients during aggregation. The objective is cast as a bi-level optimization: outer loop maximizes an attack loss L_attack over the poisoned data while the inner loop trains the victim model f_theta on clean + malicious data. Attacker goals are either system degradation (untargeted, erode overall recommendation quality, typically by platform competitors) or targeted manipulation (promote/demote specific items to specific user groups or all users, typically by product vendors). The attacker is generally assumed to treat the RS as a black box and to operate under three real-world constraints: limited knowledge (may rely on a surrogate model), bounded cost/budget for injecting fake profiles, and the need for invisibility/stealth to evade detectors. Defenders occupy the opposite side of the same model: filter malicious elements pre-training and/or harden training against those that slip through.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no empirical validation: there is no head-to-head reimplementation or unified benchmark comparing the catalogued attacks/defenses, so the taxonomy's practical discriminative value is asserted rather than measured. The metric section (Section 6) defines formulas but reports no numbers, and some formulas contain minor typographical inconsistencies (e.g., the MAE/RMSE index sets). Coverage is a curated selection (~48 attack + ~46 defense papers) rather than an exhaustive or systematic PRISMA-style review, and the emphasis is on post-2018 work with only brief treatment of earlier methods. The authors themselves note the benchmarking shortfall and the difficulty of modeling realistic adversary capabilities, which also bounds how prescriptive the defense taxonomy can be.

quantitative_results: Scope: reviews 48 poisoning-attack papers and 46 defense papers (post-2018 focus).

Targeted-attack evaluation metrics catalogued: Recnum@K (exposure count of target items in top-K), HitRatio@K (exposure rate), NDCG@K (rank-weighted target exposure), NI (increase in target-item ranks pre vs. post attack), and PS/Prediction Shift (increase in predicted rating of target items).
Untargeted-attack metrics catalogued: MAE, RMSE, Precision_rec, Recall_rec (measuring overall recommendation degradation).
Defense metrics catalogued: for poisoning filters, Precision_filter, Recall_filter, F1_filter, AUC; for robust training, the recommendation-quality metrics MAE, RMSE, Precision_rec, Recall_rec.
Attack task formalized as a bi-level optimization (outer: maximize L_attack over D_M; inner: minimize L_rec of victim f_theta on D + D_M).
Existing secure-RS benchmark platforms named as inadequate in coverage: SDLib, RecAD, ARLib.
